# Ostranauts Modding

Notes about modding the Blue Bottle Games title, [Ostranauts](https://store.steampowered.com/app/1022980/Ostranauts/). This description is valid in versions of the game from 0.8 and later. Estimated to become available in February 2022\.

## Goals of the 0.8 Modding System

1. Prevent Steam updates from wiping out mods  
2. Make writing mods easier for new mod creators  
3. Make installing mods easier/safer for players  
4. Let people pick and choose multiple mods at a time if they want

## Modding Tutorial:

To add the SampleMod.zip example mod to the game, start the game, open the Options screen, and follow the instructions below.

The Options screen can be found in-game, by pressing "OPTIONS" on the main menu, or by pressing Escape in-game and choosing "Options."  
![][image1]

1. From the Options screen, click "Files" button here to open this file, folder, and mod status screen.  
2. This will list mod names the game attempted to load, in the order they were loaded. (Usually just "Core" for the game before you've added any mods.)  
3. This will show the status of the mod after loading. "MISSING" means the mod was not found. "ERROR" means there was a problem reading one of the mod's files.  
4. Click the button next to Mods. This field shows where the game expects to find the Mods/ folder, based on the settings.json file. Clicking this button will open that folder, or if not found, will generate that folder and file, with default settings for loading the core game data (i.e. no mods).  
5. If you need this modding document again later, press this "?" button to open this document online.  
6. Keep an eye on this in-game error log. This will appear if there are issues, and turn yellow if there are important messages or warnings, and red for errors (which can break the game). Clicking it will open the log viewer for reading in-game. Log messages here will also appear in the game's data folder, inside output\_log.txt.  
7. Now that we opened the Mods/ folder in step \#4, click the red button near "Assets" above. This is where the game loads core assets from. Clicking this button opens that folder in another window.  
8. In this new file explorer window (showing StreamingAssets/), navigate up 2 folders to the folder where the Ostranauts.exe file is. ![][image2]  
9. Double-click the SampleMod.zip file (or open it with your preferred unzipping software) to examine its contents. It should contain these files: ![][image3]  
10. Drag or copy all of these files over to the file explorer window showing your Mods/ folder, like so: ![][image4]  
11. Replace the loading\_order.json file in the destination folder. The new SampleMod.zip copy will have an extra couple lines in it to load our SampleMod: ![][image5]  
12. We are mainly interested in line 7 up there, which says after loading "core," load "SampleMod" next. "SampleMod" is the folder it will look in when it comes time to load that mod. (Don't forget the comma after "core"\!)  
13. aIgnorePatterns is optional, and tells the game to skip loading anything that matches that path or filename. In this case, the game will skip loading any full names from the core data. This is useful if you want to omit core data instead of overwriting it.  
14. The SampleMod folder you copied over in step 10 above looks like this inside: ![][image6]  
15. mod\_info.json is info about your mod, like name, author, etc. ![][image7]  
16. The other folders are where your modded game data and images will go. This SampleMod changes two things. First, it modifies the appearance of the sink by providing a different sink PNG file in the images/ folder: ![][image8]  
17. The SampleMod also changes the name of the salvage tug you start the game with. It does that by including a JSON file with the modified ship data in the data/ships/ folder:   
    ![][image9]  
18. SalvagePodTut.json is a direct copy of the version from the game's core data, but with a different "model" name near the bottom: ![][image10]  
19. Now that you've copied SampleMod over, let's see if our mod shows up\!  
20. Launch the game.  
21. Click "OPTIONS" on the main menu.  
22. Click "FILES"  
23. It should look like this: ![][image11]  
24. And if you start a new game, you should see a sink with "MOD" on it like this: ![][image12]  
25. Congratulations\! You've added the SampleMod to your game.

Most mods can be installed like this, and the most you'll need to do is manually add lines to the loading\_order.json for each mod, as you saw in step 11 above. You may need to carefully choose the loading order if some mods need to load before others.

As always, feel free to ask us or community members on the Steam forums, our Discord, the BlueBottleGames.com forums, or Reddit. Folks are generally happy to help new users in each of those places\!

## Game Data Structure:

The game loads data from a set of folders within:

...\\SteamLibrary\\steamapps\\common\\Ostranauts\\Ostranauts\_Data\\StreamingAssets\\data\\

"SteamLibrary" is wherever you've chosen to install Steam library games.

Inside this data\\ folder, there is one folder per data type. It supports arbitrarily nested folders and json data within that data type.

E.g. the data\\ships\\ folder could look like:

* data\\ships\\ (JSONs)  
  * ...\\ (arbitrary nested folders, like testudo\\ or \\container\_ships\\)  
    * JSONs (myna.json, mesa.json, etc.)  
  * JSONs (myna.json, mesa.json, etc.)

Blue \= individual files  
Red \= a folder with 0 or more files/folders in it of the same type

Core data looks like this:

* path to\\Ostranauts\_Data\\StreamingAssets\\  
  * audio\\  
    * music\\ (OGGs)  
  * data\\  
    * ads\\ (JSONs) \- text for ads that appear near TV objects  
    * ai\_training\\ (JSONs) \- AI decision-making defaults.  
    * audioemitters\\ (JSONs) \- sound effect settings for items/UIs  
    * careers\\ (JSONs) \- character creation careers  
    * colors\\ (JSONs) \- common colors used in game  
    * conditions\\ (JSONs) \- stats and variables that describe an object or person. Like adjectives the game uses.  
    * conditions\_simple\\ (JSONs) \- more stats  
    * condowners\\ (JSONs) \- templates for objects in game (including people). Like nouns the game uses.  
    * condrules\\ (JSONs) \- certain conditions should trigger other conditions as they go up/down. Especially survival stats.  
    * condtrigs\\ (JSONs) \- Mostly filters for testing if a condition owner matches our needs. "TIsRed" would test if the object is red, for example. "TDn" and "TUp" entries are special cases that decrease and increase stats (used by interactions)  
    * cooverlays\\ (JSONs) \- condition owners that are variations of existing, basic ones. All of the special walls, floors, etc.  
    * crewskins\\ (JSONs) \- 3D model textures used by crew  
    * gasrespires\\ (JSONs) \- definitions for how things consume/exhale gas  
    * guipropmaps\\ (JSONs) \- mostly control panel settings and defaults  
    * headlines\\ (JSONs) \- text headlines that appear near TV objects  
    * homeworlds\\ (JSONs) \- list of possible homeworlds in the game. Used when generating humans.  
    * installables\\ (JSONs) \- list of rules for installing/uninstalling/scrapping objects  
    * interactions\\ (JSONs) \- list of actions condition owners can take. Like verbs in the game.  
    * items\\ (JSONs) \- list of visual and grid placement rules for condition owners to use.  
    * jobitems\\ (JSONs) \- special items used in gig kiosk jobs  
    * jobs\\ (JSONs) \- gig kiosk job types  
    * ledgerdefs\\ (JSONs) \- if an interaction needs to charge someone a fee, it's listed here  
    * lifeevents\\ (JSONs) \- character creation life events  
    * lights\\ (JSONs) \- lighting definitions  
    * loot\\ (JSONs) \- lists of things used by other parts of the game. Largely lists of condition owners (item loot), but can also be lists of stats, ships, images, etc.  
    * manpages\\ (JSONs) \- user manual docs  
    * music\\ (JSONs) \- soundtrack rules  
    * names\_first\\ (JSONs) \- possible given names for humans  
    * names\_last\\ (JSONs) \- possible surnames for humans  
    * names\_full\\ (JSONs) \- whole names for humans  
    * names\_ship\\ (JSONs) \- whole names for ships  
    * names\_ship\_adjectives\\ (JSONs) \- possible ship name adjectives  
    * names\_ship\_nouns\\ (JSONs) \- possible ship name nouns  
    * personspecs\\ (JSONs) \- types of NPCs we can spawn and/or search for  
    * pledges\\ (JSONs) \- special behaviors that crew (and some items, like doors) can be given  
    * powerinfos\\ (JSONs) \- power consumption and generation specifications  
    * ships\\ (JSONs) \- ship layouts/templates  
    * slots\\ (JSONs) \- gear slots on the human  
    * starsystem\\ (JSONs) \- the default star system for a new game  
    * strings\\ (JSONs) \- general strings used by game  
    * tickers\\ (JSONs) \- timers that run on condition owners  
    * traitscores\\ (JSONs) \- relative values of traits in character creation  
    * transit\\ (JSONs) \- transit destinations for each place  
  * images\\  
    * encounters\\ \- encounter art  
      * PNGs  
    * lights\\ \- sprites used for lights  
      * PNGs  
    * manuals\\  
      * per-manual-folder\\  
        * 000.png-XYZ.png  
        * PDF (optional)  
    * paperdoll\\  
      * PNGs  
    * portraits\\ \- portrait image pieces  
      * PNGs  
    * ships\\ \- preview images of ships shown in career kiosk  
      * PNGs  
    * tiles\\ \- walls and floors for ships  
      * per-tileset-folder\\  
      * PNGs  
* path to\\AppData\\LocalLow\\Blue Bottle Games\\Ostranauts\\  
  * ddsdk\\ (DeltaDNA stuff)  
  * Save1\\  
    * Captain\\ \- current save  
    * Captain.bak\\  
    * Captain.bak1\\  
    * portrait.png  
    * saveInfo.json  
  * Save2\\  
  * Save3\\  
  * Screenshots\\  
    * PNGs  
  * Unity\\ (Unity stuff)  
  * controls.json (keymapping)  
  * console\_params.json (debug console settings)  
  * settings.json (general game settings)

## Mod Files:

Also, the game will support a new Mods\\ folder, and mods will be loaded from there. By default, the game will expect it to be something like this:

...\\SteamLibrary\\steamapps\\common\\Ostranauts\\Ostranauts\_Data\\Mods\\

and have contents like this:

* mod1\\  
  * (similar to core data above)  
  * mod\_info.json  
* mod2\\  
  * (similar to core data above)  
  * mod\_info.json  
* loading\_order.json (controls order of loading mods, and which data to ignore loading)

For the most part, it is easiest to take an existing game data item (typically found in your game's ...\\Ostranauts\_Data\\StreamingAssets\\ folder), copy that file into the same location within your mod's folder, erase all the bits you don't need to change, and edit the file to include your changes.

E.g. if you wanted to modify the sink in the game to have a different name, you'd likely have a folder structure like this:

* .../Mods/  
  * loading\_order.json  
  * SinkMod/  
    * mod\_info.json  
    * data/  
      * condowners/  
        * condowners.json

And the condowners.json would just have one entry in it for the sink, but with a modified strFriendlyName value.

The loading\_order.json file in Mods\\ folder tells the game which order to load core and mod folders, using relative paths from Mods\\ folder. Items loaded later will replace similarly-named items of the same type that were loaded earlier.

In the absence of this file, the game loads core data only (default game state when purchased).

loading\_order.json also includes aIgnorePatterns, which is a list of folder/file path patterns it should skip loading, in case you want core or other mod to ignore some data, but not all data.

The settings.json file above, in the persistent data folder, controls where the game will look for this Mods\\ folder, if you wish to change where your mods are stored.

## SampleMod.zip

The game comes with a sample mod, for reference. It can be found in the game's Steam folder, and is called SampleMod.zip. This zip file includes an example loading\_order.json that loads core data first, then **SampleMod**. It also ignores loading the core data's full names json, so it only chooses names from first/last name lists.

The **SampleMod** folder in the zip is a simple mod that changes the appearance of the sink's sprite to have "MOD" scrawled on it, and the starting salvage tug is named "Modded TU-77a" instead of "TU-77a".
